articles.title: Knowledge Distillation of Black-Box Large Language Models
articles.summary: Knowledge distillation of black-box large language models has been a topic of increasing research in recent years, with the goal of boosting the capabilities of smaller models through knowledge transfer from powerful yet black-box teachers. A novel method called Proxy-KD uses a proxy model to facilitate efficient knowledge transfer, and experiments show that it not only enhances performance but also surpasses traditional white-box techniques.

articles.title: A QNX-inspired operating system with selectable kernels

The operating system runs on Skimmer, a microkernel written from scratch for this project, and uses seL4 as its kernel. Both variants offer the kernel and initrd files directly usable in e.g. U-Boot loader.

articles.title: Do LLMs pass the mirror test?
articles.summary: LLMs pass the mirror test in some cases but not others, with many models failing to accurately identify their responses among an anonymized lineup. This is because LLMs are primarily text-based and do not rely on visual cues like dogs do when testing self-recognition through the mirror test. The test measures anomaly detection against an internal baseline, which can be achieved through other means such as identifying discrepancies in a model's output or using external data to verify its responses.

articles.title: Examining circuit boards from the Space Shuttle's I/O Processor
articles.summary: The Space Shuttle's I/O Processor consisted of two 60-pound aluminum-alloy boxes that housed a 32-bit processor and implemented input/output capabilities, including 24 high-speed networks connecting to sensors and systems. This programmable computer had an unusual architecture with 25 virtual processors running on one physical processor, making it more complicated than the main CPU. The I/O Processor's circuit cards included network interface pages with four network connections each, storing microcode bits in tiny metal fuses programmed by blowing a fuse for each bit.

articles.title: I used Claude Code to get a second opinion on my MRI

The analysis revealed that I had a Grade III partial-thickness tear at the apical insertion of my subscapularis tendon, but this diagnosis was not supported by the MRI results or treatment suggested by the clinic. This experience highlights the importance of having a second opinion and being aware of potential biases in medical technology.

articles.title: Towards Understandable Software
articles.summary: Programming can be unapproachable due to conflicting software stacks, endless boilerplate, and annoying libraries, making it difficult for developers to create interesting code. However, this doesn't mean that Large Language Models (LLMs) are inherently bad, as they have been used effectively in industry software development. The problem lies in the fact that LLMs often perpetuate existing problems such as environmental destruction, inconsistent code, and dependency issues. By recognizing these limitations, developers can work towards creating more maintainable systems by tackling the underlying software stack rather than just automating one layer of abstraction.

articles.title: Regular expressions that work “everywhere”
articles.summary: Regular expressions that work everywhere are a common frustration for developers who use them across multiple tools and platforms. One way to address this is by using Perl analogs of other tools, such as sed, awk, grep, and Emacs, which have similar features but with slightly different syntax. The stricter definition of "everywhere" the more it includes, while a less strict definition would include tools like word boundaries in awk or punctuation characters in Emacs. By identifying a subset of regex features that work everywhere, developers can create code that works out of the box across multiple platforms and tools.

articles.title: How VictoriaLogs Stores Your Logs in a Columnar Layout
articles.summary: VictoriaLogs stores logs in a columnar layout by translating log lines into a single internal shape that the rest of the system understands, which includes a timestamp, named fields, and a stream identity. This process is influenced by query arguments or headers such as ignore_fields, decolorize_fields, extra_fields, _msg, _time_field, _stream_fields, and VL-Stream-Fields. By keeping each stream's logs together on disk, VictoriaLogs optimizes compression and allows queries to touch only the streams they need instead of scanning everything.
